questions.text: Which of these increase the movement of sap within the xylem?I. Water moves into guard cells’ vacuoles.II. Humidity increases in surrounding air.III. Root endodermis prevents ions from entering xylem.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Correct. Water moving into guard cells increases turgor pressure, causing them to swell. This opens the stomatal pores, facilitating transpiration and drawing water up through the xylem.
Incorrect. Increasing humidity reduces transpiration by decreasing the water vapor gradient between the leaf and surrounding air, thus not enhancing the movement of sap within the xylem.
Incorrect. While the root endodermis plays a role in regulating ion entry into the xylem, preventing ions from entering would not increase sap movement.
Incorrect. Only the first statement is correct. The root endodermis preventing ions from entering the xylem would not enhance sap movement.

questions.explanation: The only process that directly leads to an increase in the movement of sap within the xylem is Water moves into guard cells’ vacuoles (I) (A).The primary mechanism for the movement of sap in the xylem is the transpiration pull (Cohesion-Tension theory). The water moves into the guard cells, making them turgid and causing the stomata to open. Open stomata increase the rate of transpiration (water evaporation from the leaves). This rapid water loss creates a strong tension (negative pressure) that pulls the continuous column of water and sap up the xylem vessels from the roots to the leaves, thus increasing the rate of movement.

questions.text: The most abundant solute in the phloem sap is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While potassium ions (K+) may be present in the phloem sap, they are not the most abundant solute. Potassium ions play important roles in various physiological processes within plants, such as osmoregulation, enzyme activation, and stomatal regulation. However, in terms of abundance, sucrose is typically the primary solute found in the phloem sap.

This statement is correct. Sucrose is the most abundant solute in the phloem sap. Sucrose is a disaccharide composed of glucose and fructose. It is produced in the source tissues, mainly in the leaves through photosynthesis. Sucrose is actively transported into the sieve tubes of the phloem and serves as the main form of sugar used for long-distance translocation within the plant. It is transported from the source to sink tissues, where it is utilized for growth, energy, or storage.

While glucose is an important sugar in plant metabolism, it is not typically the most abundant solute in the phloem sap. Glucose is a monosaccharide and serves as a primary energy source within cells. It can be produced during photosynthesis or derived from the breakdown of other sugars. However, in the phloem sap, sucrose is more commonly found as the primary transported sugar.

Starch is not typically found as a solute in the phloem sap. Starch is a polysaccharide and serves as a storage form of glucose in plants. It is commonly found in storage organs such as tubers, roots, and seeds. Starch is converted into sucrose before being transported through the phloem for long-distance translocation. Therefore, while starch may be present in certain plant tissues, it is not the most abundant solute in the phloem sap.

questions.explanation: Sucrose is the most abundant solute in the phloem sap. Sucrose is a disaccharide composed of glucose and fructose. It is produced in the source tissues, mainly in the leaves, through photosynthesis. Sucrose is actively transported into the sieve tubes of the phloem and serves as the main form of sugar used for long-distance translocation within the plant. It is transported from the source to sink tissues, where it is utilized for growth, energy, or storage.

questions.text: Exceptionally, the positive hydrostatic pressure generated by root pressure is about how many Kpa?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hydrostatic pressure is the pressure exerted by any fluid at resting or equilibrium at any point due to gravitational force. When the force is exerted towards the gravity it is known as positive hydrostatic pressure. When the force is exerted opposite to gravity it is known as negative hydrostatic pressure. It has been estimated that a positive hydrostatic pressure of around 100 to 200 kilo Pascal exceptionally 800 kilo Pascal is generated by root pressure.

Hydrostatic pressure is the pressure exerted by any fluid at resting or equilibrium at any point due to gravitational force. When the force is exerted towards the gravity it is known as positive hydrostatic pressure. When the force is exerted opposite to gravity it is known as negative hydrostatic pressure. It has been estimated that a positive hydrostatic pressure of around 100 to 200 kilo Pascal exceptionally 800 kilo Pascal is generated by root pressure.

Hydrostatic pressure is the pressure exerted by any fluid at resting or equilibrium at any point due to gravitational force. When the force is exerted towards the gravity it is known as positive hydrostatic pressure. When the force is exerted opposite to gravity it is known as negative hydrostatic pressure. It has been estimated that a positive hydrostatic pressure of around 100 to 200 kilo Pascal exceptionally 800 kilo Pascal is generated by root pressure.

Hydrostatic pressure is the pressure exerted by any fluid at resting or equilibrium at any point due to gravitational force. When the force is exerted towards the gravity it is known as positive hydrostatic pressure. When the force is exerted opposite to gravity it is known as negative hydrostatic pressure. It has been estimated that a positive hydrostatic pressure of around 100 to 200 kilo Pascal exceptionally 800 kilo Pascal is generated by root pressure.

questions.explanation: It has been estimated that a positive hydrostatic pressure of around 100 to 200 KPa (exceptionally 800 KPa) is generated by root pressure.

questions.text: During transport of carbon dioxide, blood does not become acidic due to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: When carbon dioxide enters the blood, most of it is converted into carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), which dissociates into H⁺ and HCO₃⁻. The blood has an efficient buffer system, mainly the bicarbonate buffer (H₂CO₃/HCO₃⁻ pair), along with proteins and hemoglobin that bind excess H⁺. This prevents large fluctuations in pH and keeps blood from becoming too acidic during CO₂ transport.

This is incorrect because sodium carbonate (Na₂CO₃) is not present in blood under normal physiological conditions. The neutralization of carbonic acid by Na₂CO₃ does not occur in the body. This option is implausible scientifically.

Leukocytes (white blood cells) are primarily involved in immunity and defense against pathogens. They do not play a role in neutralizing or transporting carbon dioxide, nor in controlling blood pH. Hence, this is incorrect.

It is true that CO₂ does not usually accumulate in blood under normal breathing, but this is not the reason why blood does not become acidic. Even small amounts of CO₂ produce H⁺ ions, and without buffering, pH would drop. Therefore, this option is misleading and incorrect.

questions.explanation: When CO₂ diffuses into the blood from tissues, much of it is converted to bicarbonate ions through the action of carbonic anhydrase in red blood cells. This process generates hydrogen ions, which could make the blood acidic. However, the blood is protected by an efficient buffer system, mainly the bicarbonate buffer and hemoglobin, which bind and neutralize free H⁺ ions, maintaining pH near 7.4. Other proposed mechanisms such as neutralization by sodium carbonate, leukocyte absorption, or simple non-accumulation are either physiologically inaccurate or insufficient. Thus, the blood buffer system is the correct reason why blood does not become acidic during CO₂ transport.

questions.text: Blood pressure in the mammalian aorta is maximum during
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During the systole of the left ventricle, the heart contracts, pushing blood into the aorta, resulting in the highest blood pressure.
During diastole of the right atrium, the heart is in a relaxed state, allowing it to fill with blood. This phase does not affect the aorta's blood pressure significantly.
The systole of the left atrium involves contraction to move blood into the left ventricle, but it does not directly influence the blood pressure in the aorta significantly.
During diastole of the right ventricle, the heart is in a relaxed state, allowing it to fill with blood from the right atrium. This does not impact the aorta's blood pressure.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the systole of the left ventricle. During this phase, the left ventricle contracts forcefully, pushing blood into the aorta, which causes a temporary rise in blood pressure known as systolic pressure. The aorta, being the main artery, directly feels this increase in pressure. The other options involve phases of the cardiac cycle that do not directly affect the blood pressure in the aorta. The diastole of the right atrium and right ventricle are related to the filling of these chambers and do not influence aortic pressure. The systole of the left atrium is mainly responsible for pushing blood into the left ventricle and has little direct impact on aortic pressure.

questions.text: A fluid in transit between interstitial fluid and the blood
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: Lymph is the fluid that circulates throughout the lymphatic system and is formed when the interstitial fluid is collected through lymph capillaries. The lymph ultimately mixes back with blood by travelling into the right or the left subclavian.

questions.text: Amphibians generally have three chambers in their hearts. What type of chambers they are?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct. Amphibians generally have a heart with three chambers, consisting of one ventricle and two atria.
This option is incorrect. Amphibians do not have two ventricles; instead, they have one ventricle and two atria.
This option is incorrect. Amphibians have two atria, not one.

options.text: One ventricle, one atrium, one sinus venosus
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While the sinus venosus is part of the heart structure in amphibians, it is not considered a chamber in the same way as the atria and ventricle are.

questions.explanation: Amphibians typically have a heart with three chambers, which includes one ventricle and two atria. This structure allows for some mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood, which is suitable for their metabolism and lifestyle. In contrast, the hearts of birds and mammals have four chambers (two atria and two ventricles) to completely separate oxygenated and deoxygenated blood. The incorrect options either suggest an incorrect number of chambers or misidentify the sinus venosus as a primary chamber.

questions.text: Which of the following is known as the vessels of the vessels?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Vasa vasorum is a network of small blood vessels that supply oxygen and nutrients to the walls of larger blood vessels, such as arteries and veins. The term "vasa vasorum" literally means "vessels of the vessels" in Latin, as they provide nourishment to the vessels themselves.
This is the middle layer of a blood vessel wall, composed of smooth muscle and elastic fibers. While it's an important part of the vessel wall, it's not referred to as the "vessels of the vessels".
This is the outermost layer of a blood vessel wall, also known as the adventitia. It provides support and protection to the vessel, but it's not the "vessels of the vessels".
This is the innermost layer of a blood vessel wall, also known as the intima. It's in contact with the blood flowing through the vessel, but it's not referred to as the "vessels of the vessels".

questions.explanation: Vasa vasorum is a network of small blood vessels that supply oxygen and nutrients to the walls of larger blood vessels, such as arteries and veins. The term "vasa vasorum" literally means "vessels of the vessels" in Latin, as they provide nourishment to the vessels themselves.

questions.text: The frog’s heart, when taken out of the body, continues to beat for sometime.Select the best reason for this from the following statements. (1) The frog is a poikilotherm. (2) The frog does not have any coronary circulation. (3) Its heart is “myogenic” in nature. (4) Its heart is autoexcitable.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: (3) is also correct because frog's heart is myogenic too.
Frog is homeotherm. Frog does have coronary circulation.

The frog’s heart is myogenic, i.e. its heartbeat originates from muscles of heart. Hence, it is autorythmic. A wave of contraction originates from the sinus venosus and spreads to the wall of sinus venosus and both auricles. This compels the heart to beat. Due to this reason, the frog’s heart will continue to beat as long as it gets a supply of ATP.
(4) is also correct beacuse frog's heart is autoexcitable.

questions.explanation: The frog’s heart is myogenic, i.e. its heartbeat originates from muscles of heart. Hence, it is autorythmic. A wave of contraction originates from the sinus venosus and spreads to the wall of sinus venosus and both auricles. This compels the heart to beat. Due to this reason, the frog’s heart will continue to beat as long as it gets a supply of ATP.

questions.text: In mammals, which blood vessel would normally carry the largest amount of urea?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The hepatic vein is responsible for carrying blood away from the liver. Since the liver is the primary site for the urea cycle, the hepatic vein carries a high concentration of urea as it exits the liver.
The hepatic portal vein carries nutrient-rich blood from the digestive organs to the liver, but it does not carry a high concentration of urea as the urea cycle primarily occurs in the liver.
The renal vein carries filtered blood away from the kidneys. It contains less urea because the kidneys filter urea out of the blood into the urine.

The function of the pulmonary vein is to transport oxygen-rich (oxygenated) blood from the lungs back to the heart. This freshly oxygenated blood enters the left atrium, the heart's upper left chamber, where it is then circulated to the rest of the body.

questions.explanation: Urea is synthesized in the liver during the urea cycle. The hepatic vein drains blood from the liver, so it contains the highest concentration of urea.

questions.text: The cardiac pacemaker in a patient fails to function normally. The doctors find that an artificial pacemaker is to be grafted in him.It is likely that it will be grafted at the site of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: AV bundle/Bundle of His dosen't act as a pacemaker.
Purkinje fibres don't act as a pacemaker, they are present to transfer action potential.
Artificial pacemaker is to be always grafted at sinuatrial node because SA node acts as a pacemaker. .
SA node acts as a natural pacemaker, not AV node. That's why artificial pacemaker has to be installed in SA node only.

questions.explanation: SA (sinoatrial) node is a specialized bundle of neurons located in the upper part of the right atrium of the heart. SA node is the natural cardiac pacemaker from which the heartbeat originates. If this system is damaged, it may send non­coordinated impulses to the heart chambers resulting in symptoms like irregular heart rate, tiredness, dizziness, and loss of consciousness. As the pacemaker cells create this rhythmical impulse therefore an artificial pacemaker is implanted at the site of the SA node to mimic the actions of the node and conducting system and helps to regulate heartbeat.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct about the heart?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The right ventricle pumps blood to the aorta
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The right ventricle does not pump blood to the aorta. The right ventricle's primary function is to pump deoxygenated blood to the pulmonary artery. The pulmonary artery carries the deoxygenated blood to the lungs, where it picks up oxygen and undergoes oxygenation.

options.text: The left ventricle pumps blood to the entire body
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The left ventricle is responsible for pumping oxygenated blood to the entire body through the systemic circulation. It receives oxygenated blood from the left atrium and contracts forcefully to propel the blood out of the heart and into the aorta. From the aorta, the oxygenated blood is distributed to the organs and tissues throughout the body.

options.text: The right atrium receives oxygenated blood from the lungs
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from the body through the superior and inferior vena cava. The blood returning from the body is low in oxygen and high in carbon dioxide. It then passes through the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle.

options.text: The right ventricle pumps blood to the right atrium
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The right ventricle does not pump blood to the right atrium. Instead, the right ventricle receives deoxygenated blood from the right atrium and contracts to pump it into the pulmonary artery, which carries the blood to the lungs for oxygenation.

questions.explanation: The left ventricle is responsible for pumping oxygenated blood to the entire body through the systemic circulation. It receives oxygenated blood from the left atrium and contracts forcefully to propel the blood out of the heart and into the aorta. From the aorta, the oxygenated blood is distributed to the organs and tissues throughout the body.

questions.text: All of the following are correct about human red blood cells. EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is correct. Red blood cells have an average lifespan of approximately 120 days. After this period, they are removed from circulation and broken down by the spleen and liver.

This statement is incorrect. Leukocytes are a different type of blood cell known as white blood cells. They play a crucial role in the immune response and protection against infections. Red blood cells are called erythrocytes.

options.text: They do not have a nucleus
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Mature red blood cells do not have a nucleus. During their development, red blood cells expel their nucleus to make space for more hemoglobin, the protein responsible for carrying oxygen.

options.text: They are formed in the bone marrow
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Red blood cells are formed in the bone marrow through a process called erythropoiesis. Stem cells in the bone marrow differentiate and mature into red blood cells under the influence of a hormone called erythropoietin.

questions.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Leukocytes are a different type of blood cell known as white blood cells. They play a crucial role in the immune response and protection against infections. Red blood cells are called erythrocytes.

questions.text: All of the following are correct about human circulation EXCEPT
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is correct. Arteries are blood vessels that carry oxygenated blood away from the heart and distribute it to the various tissues and organs of the body. Arteries have thick muscular walls that allow them to withstand the high pressure generated by the heart's contractions and to regulate blood flow.

options.text: The pacemaker of the heart is the SA node
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The sinoatrial (SA) node, located in the right atrium of the heart, is often referred to as the natural pacemaker of the heart. It initiates the electrical signals that regulate the heart's rhythm and sets the pace for the heartbeat.

This statement is correct. Leukocytes, also known as white blood cells, play a crucial role in the immune response and defense against disease. They are responsible for identifying and attacking pathogens, foreign substances, and abnormal cells in the body.

options.text: The only artery carrying deoxygenated blood is the aorta
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The aorta is an artery that carries oxygenated blood. It receives oxygenated blood from the left ventricle of the heart and distributes it to the rest of the body. The pulmonary artery, on the other hand, is the only artery in the body that carries deoxygenated blood. It transports deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle of the heart to the lungs for oxygenation.

questions.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The aorta is an artery that carries oxygenated blood. It receives oxygenated blood from the left ventricle of the heart and distributes it to the rest of the body. The pulmonary artery, on the other hand, is the only artery in the body that carries deoxygenated blood. It transports deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle of the heart to the lungs for oxygenation.

questions.text: All of the following are related to blood clotting EXCEPT
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Fibrinogen is a plasma protein that plays a critical role in blood clotting. When there is an injury or damage to blood vessels, fibrinogen is converted into fibrin by the action of thrombin. Fibrin forms a mesh-like structure, which helps in clot formation by trapping platelets and other blood components.
Erythrocytes, also known as red blood cells, are not directly involved in the process of blood clotting. Their primary function is to carry oxygen to the body's tissues and remove carbon dioxide.

Thromboplastin, also known as tissue factor, is an enzyme that is released from damaged tissues or activated platelets during the clotting process. It initiates a series of reactions known as the coagulation cascade, leading to the formation of a blood clot.

Calcium ions are essential for the process of blood clotting. They play a crucial role in several steps of the coagulation cascade, including the activation of specific clotting factors and the conversion of prothrombin to thrombin, which is necessary for the formation of fibrin.

questions.explanation: Erythrocytes, also known as red blood cells, are not directly involved in the process of blood clotting. Their primary function is to carry oxygen to the body's tissues and remove carbon dioxide.

questions.text: All of the following are correct about the normal direction of blood flow EXCEPT
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The correct direction of blood flow is from the vena cava to the right atrium, not directly to the right ventricle. The vena cavae are large veins that carry deoxygenated blood from the body to the right atrium. From the right atrium, blood flows through the tricuspid valve to reach the right ventricle.

This statement is correct. Blood flows from the right atrium to the right ventricle through the tricuspid valve. The tricuspid valve is located between these two chambers of the heart and ensures that blood flows in the correct direction, preventing backflow.

This statement is correct. After the left ventricle contracts, blood is pumped out through the aortic semilunar valve. This valve opens to allow blood to flow from the left ventricle into the aorta, the largest artery in the body. From the aorta, oxygenated blood is distributed to the rest of the body.

This statement is correct. After blood is oxygenated in the lungs, it returns to the heart through the pulmonary veins. The pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium of the heart. From the left atrium, blood then flows through the mitral valve (bicuspid valve) to enter the left ventricle.

questions.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The correct direction of blood flow is from the vena cava to the right atrium, not directly to the right ventricle. The vena cavae are large veins that carry deoxygenated blood from the body to the right atrium. From the right atrium, blood flows through the tricuspid valve to reach the right ventricle.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly follows the flow of blood in the human body?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Right ventricle → aorta → body → left atrium → left ventricle → pulmonary artery
options.explanation: This sequence is not correct. The blood from the right ventricle should go to the pulmonary artery, not the aorta, before it is pumped to the lungs.

options.text: Right ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary vein → left atrium
options.explanation: This is the correct sequence. Blood from the right ventricle is pumped into the pulmonary artery, which carries it to the lungs. In the lungs, the blood is oxygenated, then it returns to the heart via the pulmonary veins, entering the left atrium.So, it is the correct sequence as the blood flows from the right ventricle to the pulmonary artery, then to the lungs, and finally back to the left atrium via the pulmonary vein.

options.text: Left atrium → left ventricle → pulmonary artery → pulmonary vein → right atrium
options.explanation: This sequence is not correct. It starts with the left side of the heart and ends with the right side, which is the reverse of the normal blood flow.

options.text: Left atrium → left ventricle → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary vein → right atrium
options.explanation: This option also reverses the normal blood flow. It starts with the left side of the heart and ends with the right side.

questions.explanation: This is the correct sequence. Blood from the right ventricle is pumped into the pulmonary artery, which carries it to the lungs. In the lungs, the blood is oxygenated, then it returns to the heart via the pulmonary veins, entering the left atrium.So, it is the correct sequence as the blood flows from the right ventricle to the pulmonary artery, then to the lungs, and finally back to the left atrium via the pulmonary vein.

questions.text: The largest amount of the carbon dioxide is carried to the lungs by
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Leukocytes are white blood cells, and their primary function is to fight infection and provide immune defence. They are not directly involved in the transport of carbon dioxide in the bloodstream. Their main role is to combat pathogens and maintain the body's immune system.

Thrombocytes are also known as platelets. They are involved in the blood-clotting process and help to stop bleeding when there is an injury. Platelets do not have a direct role in the transport of carbon dioxide.

Haemoglobin in red blood cells can indeed bind to carbon dioxide, but the largest amount of carbon dioxide in the bloodstream is carried in the form of bicarbonate ions in the plasma. Hemoglobin's role in carbon dioxide transport is significant, but it is not the primary mechanism for carrying the largest amount of carbon dioxide.

options.text: The plasma as the bicarbonate ion
options.explanation: This is the correct option. The largest amount of carbon dioxide is transported in the bloodstream as bicarbonate ions dissolved in the plasma. When carbon dioxide is produced in the body's tissues, it combines with water to form carbonic acid, which then dissociates into bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) and hydrogen ions (H+). The bicarbonate ions are transported in the blood to the lungs, where they release carbon dioxide, which is then exhaled. This bicarbonate ion transport is a crucial mechanism for efficiently moving carbon dioxide from the tissues to the lungs for removal.

questions.explanation: The largest amount of carbon dioxide is transported in the bloodstream as bicarbonate ions dissolved in the plasma. When carbon dioxide is produced in the body's tissues, it combines with water to form carbonic acid, which then dissociates into bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) and hydrogen ions (H+). The bicarbonate ions are transported in the blood to the lungs, where they release carbon dioxide, which is then exhaled. This bicarbonate ion transport is a crucial mechanism for efficiently moving carbon dioxide from the tissues to the lungs for removal.

questions.text: All of the following are true about blood EXCEPT
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: White blood cells are formed in the bone marrow
options.explanation: This statement is true. White blood cells, or leukocytes, are produced in the bone marrow. They are a crucial part of the body's immune system and play a role in fighting infections and providing immune defence.

options.text: Platelets are not cells but are cell fragments
options.explanation: This statement is true. Platelets, also known as thrombocytes, are not complete cells but rather small cell fragments. They are essential for blood clotting and help in the process of hemostasis.

options.text: Arteries contain valves to help in the pumping of blood
options.explanation: This statement is not true. Arteries do not contain valves. Valves are typically found in veins, where they prevent the backflow of blood and ensure that it flows in one direction, especially in areas where blood needs to move against gravity. Arteries have thicker and more muscular walls to carry blood away from the heart.

options.text: The liquid portion of the blood is called plasma
options.explanation: This statement is true. The liquid component of blood is called plasma. Plasma is a pale yellowish fluid that makes up about 55% of total blood volume and contains water, electrolytes, proteins, hormones, waste products, and various other substances.

questions.explanation: This statement is not true. Arteries do not contain valves. Valves are typically found in veins, where they prevent the backflow of blood and ensure that it flows in one direction, especially in areas where blood needs to move against gravity. Arteries have thicker and more muscular walls to carry blood away from the heart.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about blood is FALSE?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Mature red blood cells are not nucleated
options.explanation: This statement is true. Mature red blood cells, or erythrocytes, do not have a nucleus. In their maturation process, they expel their nucleus to make more space for hemoglobin, the oxygen-carrying protein. This lack of a nucleus allows them to have a biconcave shape and be more efficient at carrying oxygen.

options.text: Blood platelets are involved in the clotting process
options.explanation: This statement is true. Blood platelets, or thrombocytes, are essential for blood clotting. When there is an injury or damage to blood vessels, platelets aggregate at the site to form a plug, and they release clotting factors to help form a blood clot, which stops bleeding.

options.text: The adult spleen is a site of red blood cell development
options.explanation: This statement is FALSE. In adults, the spleen is not a site of red blood cell development. Red blood cells are primarily produced in the bone marrow throughout an individual's life. The spleen's role is to filter and remove old or damaged red blood cells from circulation and to help with immune function.

options.text: White blood cells are capable of phagocytosising foreign matter
options.explanation: This statement is true. White blood cells, or leukocytes, are part of the body's immune system and can phagocytose (engulf and digest) foreign matter such as bacteria, viruses, and other microorganisms. This is an important part of their role in protecting the body against infections.

questions.explanation: This statement is FALSE. In adults, the spleen is not a site of red blood cell development. Red blood cells are primarily produced in the bone marrow throughout an individual's life. The spleen's role is to filter and remove old or damaged red blood cells from circulation and to help with immune function.

questions.text: Which of the following is a normal pathway of blood flow?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is not the normal pathway of blood flow. Blood from the right ventricle is typically pumped into the pulmonary artery, which carries it to the lungs for oxygenation. From the lungs, oxygenated blood returns to the left atrium.

This is part of the normal pathway of blood flow. Oxygenated blood from the lungs flows through the pulmonary veins and enters the left atrium of the heart. From the left atrium, it moves into the left ventricle and is then pumped out to the rest of the body through the aorta.

options.text: Inferior vena cava to left atrium
options.explanation: This is not the normal pathway of blood flow. The inferior vena cava carries deoxygenated blood from the lower half of the body (and the superior vena cava from the upper half of the body) to the right atrium, not the left atrium.

This is not the normal pathway of blood flow. Blood flows from the left atrium to the left ventricle before being pumped out to the body through the aorta. The pulmonary veins transport oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium, not directly to the left ventricle.

questions.explanation: This is part of the normal pathway of blood flow. Oxygenated blood from the lungs flows through the pulmonary veins and enters the left atrium of the heart. From the left atrium, it moves into the left ventricle and is then pumped out to the rest of the body through the aorta.

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct sequence of a cardiac impulse?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: SA node → AV node → Purkinje fibres → bundle of His → ventricles
options.explanation: This sequence is incorrect because the impulse travels from the AV node to the bundle of His before reaching the Purkinje fibres.

options.text: AV node → bundle of His → Purkinje fibres → ventricles → atria
options.explanation: This sequence is incorrect because the initial impulse originates in the SA node, not the AV node, and the final destination is the ventricles, with no backflow to the atria.

options.text: SA node → atria → AV node → bundle of His → Purkinje fibres → ventricles
options.explanation: This sequence correctly shows the impulse beginning at the SA node, spreading across the atria, reaching the AV node, and then rapidly descending through the specialized ventricular pathways.

options.text: SA node → AV node → atria → bundle of His → Purkinje fibres → ventricles
options.explanation: This sequence incorrectly places the contraction of the atria after the signal has already passed through the AV node. The atrial contraction happens immediately after the SA node fires.

questions.explanation: The electrical impulse that starts the heartbeat is generated by the Sinoatrial (SA) node, the heart's natural pacemaker. This impulse immediately spreads throughout the muscle of the atria, causing them to contract and push blood into the ventricles. The signal then reaches the Atrioventricular (AV) node, where it is delayed slightly to allow the atria to fully empty. From the AV node, the impulse moves quickly into the bundle of His (also called the AV bundle) and then down the interventricular septum, where it rapidly distributes through the Purkinje fibres to the muscle walls of the ventricles, causing them to contract and pump blood out of the heart.

questions.text: Haemoglobin’s affinity for O2
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During exercise, muscle tissue requires more oxygen for energy production. This increased demand for oxygen triggers several physiological responses, including an increase in temperature, a decrease in pH, and an increase in carbon dioxide production. These changes in the local environment of the muscle tissue cause a decrease in haemoglobin's affinity for oxygen, allowing it to release oxygen more readily to the exercising muscles. So this option is not correct.

An increase in blood PCO2, which occurs when there is an accumulation of carbon dioxide in the blood, leads to a decrease in haemoglobin's affinity for oxygen. This phenomenon is known as the Bohr effect. Conversely, when blood PCO2 decreases (such as during hyperventilation or in certain respiratory disorders), the affinity of haemoglobin for oxygen increases. This option is not correct.

A decrease in blood pH, which indicates an increase in acidity, leads to a decrease in haemoglobin's affinity for oxygen. This is known as the Bohr effect as well. When blood pH decreases (becomes more acidic), such as during exercise or in conditions like acidosis, haemoglobin's affinity for oxygen decreases, allowing for the release of oxygen to the tissues. This is the correct one.

options.text: Is higher in maternal blood than in foetal blood.
options.explanation: The affinity of haemoglobin for oxygen is actually higher in foetal blood than in maternal blood. This is due to a difference in the structure of foetal haemoglobin (HbF) compared to adult haemoglobin (HbA). Foetal haemoglobin has a higher affinity for oxygen, which facilitates the transfer of oxygen from the mother's bloodstream across the placenta to the foetus. This higher affinity allows the foetus to efficiently extract oxygen from the maternal blood supply.

questions.explanation: A decrease in blood pH, which indicates an increase in acidity, leads to a decrease in haemoglobin's affinity for oxygen. This is known as the Bohr effect as well. When blood pH decreases (becomes more acidic), such as during exercise or in conditions like acidosis, haemoglobin's affinity for oxygen decreases, allowing for the release of oxygen to the tissues.

questions.text: The sino-atrial node is the normal pacemaker of the human heart because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It is located in the right atrium
options.explanation: The SA node is a small cluster of specialised cells located in the upper part of the right atrium near the opening of the superior vena cava. However, it is not the reason for which it's considered a pacemaker.

options.text: It is the only part of the conducting system that can generate impulse
options.explanation: The SA node is the only part of the cardiac conducting system that can generate an electrical impulse spontaneously. It has intrinsic automaticity, meaning it can generate electrical impulses without requiring external stimulation. This property allows the SA node to set the pace for the rest of the heart, leading to coordinated contractions. This option is correct however, option C is the most suitable.

options.text: It can generate the maximum number of action potentials in the conducting system
options.explanation: This option is the correct answer. The SA node can generate the maximum number of action potentials in the conducting system. It has a natural firing rate of around 60-100 action potentials per minute, which is faster than other parts of the conducting system. This high firing rate ensures a regular and coordinated pattern of electrical signals that drive the heart's contractions.

options.text: It produces action potentials that are more in size than the other parts of the conducting system
options.explanation: This option is not accurate. The size of action potentials generated by the SA node is not significantly different from those generated by other parts of the conducting system. The primary distinction of the SA node is its ability to generate impulses spontaneously, not the size of the action potentials it produces.

questions.explanation: The SA node can generate the maximum number of action potentials in the conducting system. It has a natural firing rate of around 60-100 action potentials per minute, which is faster than other parts of the conducting system. This high firing rate ensures a regular and coordinated pattern of electrical signals that drive the heart's contractions.

questions.text: The given figure shows a schematic plan of blood circulation in humans with labels A to D. Identify the labels along with their functions and select the correct option.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A-Pulmonary vein - takes impure blood from body parts to heart, Po2 = 60 mm Hg
options.explanation: This describes label A as the pulmonary vein carrying impure blood, but it actually brings oxygen-rich blood from the lungs to the heart. The oxygen pressure value of 60mm Hg is also much lower than the actual value found in this vessel.

options.text: B-Pulmonary artery - takes blood from heart to lungs, Po2 90 mm Hg
options.explanation: This suggests that label B is the pulmonary artery, but the diagram shows B carrying blood away from the heart to the lower body tissues as the aorta. Additionally, the pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood, which would have a much lower oxygen pressure than 90mmHg.

options.text: C-Vena Cava - takes blood from body parts to right atrium, Pco2= 45 mm Hg
options.explanation: This is correct because label C represents the vena cava, which collects used blood from body parts and returns it to the right atrium. It accurately states that the carbon dioxide pressure in this deoxygenated blood is approximately 45mmHg.

options.text: D-Dorsal aorta - takes blood from heart to body parts, Poz 95 mm Hg
options.explanation: This labels D as the dorsal aorta, but D is actually the pulmonary artery because it is shown carrying blood toward the lungs. While the aorta does have high oxygen levels, it is represented by label B in this specific circulatory plan.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is c because label C accurately identifies the vena cava, which returns carbon dioxide-rich blood from the body to the right atrium at a pressure of 45mm Hg. The other options are incorrect because they misname the blood vessels or provide wrong partial pressure values for the oxygen and carbon dioxide they carry. This schematic highlights how the right side of the heart handles deoxygenated blood while the left side manages oxygenated blood.

questions.text: Which structures carry blood rich in oxygen?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These structures do not carry oxygenated blood.
Although 5 and 7 are correct, 3 isn't.
4 and 6 do not have oxygenated blood.

1 is pulmonary vein which brings oxygenated blood from lungs. 5 is the left atrium, where the pulmonary vein gives off the oxygenated blood. 7 is the left ventricle which then receives oxygenated blood.

questions.explanation: 1 is pulmonary vein which brings oxygenated blood from lungs. 5 is the left atrium, where the pulmonary vein gives off the oxygenated blood. 7 is the left ventricle which then receives oxygenated blood.

questions.text: Which structure returns blood poor in oxygen to the heart?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This part represents the aorta, which is the largest artery in the body. Its main function is to carry oxygenated blood away from the left ventricle and distribute it to all the organs and tissues.
This structure is the pulmonary artery. It functions to transport deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs so that it can release carbon dioxide and pick up fresh oxygen.
This is the vena cava, consisting of superior and inferior branches. Its primary function is to return oxygen-poor blood from the various parts of the body back into the right atrium of the heart.
These are the pulmonary veins. Their role is to carry oxygen-rich blood from the lungs and return it to the left atrium so it can be pumped out to the rest of the body.

questions.explanation: The structure that returns oxygen-poor blood to the heart is the vena cava, which is labeled as 3 in the image. While the pulmonary veins (labeled 4) also return blood to the heart, they carry blood that is already oxygenated from the lungs. The aorta and pulmonary artery (labeled 1 and 2) are not correct because they are arteries that carry blood away from the heart rather than returning it.

questions.text: Tissue that gives rise to all vascular tissue
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Xylem is a type of vascular tissue; it transports water and minerals but does not give rise to new vascular tissue.
This is incorrect. Phloem is a type of vascular tissue; it transports sugars but does not generate new vascular tissue.
Vascular cambium is oriented directly between xylem and phloem. It is a meristematic tissue that gives rise to both types of vascular tissue. Hence, this option is correct.

This is incorrect. Bark is the outermost protective covering of the woody stem, made up of various tissues including cork and secondary phloem; it is not the meristematic tissue that produces the vascular tissue.

questions.explanation: The tissue that gives rise to all vascular tissue (in secondary growth) is the Cambium (C).The Cambium is a thin strip of perpetually dividing cells (a lateral meristem) found in the stems and roots of dicots and gymnosperms. This layer is specifically called the Vascular Cambium. Its primary function is to divide: cells produced toward the inside differentiate into secondary xylem, and cells produced toward the outside differentiate into secondary phloem. Therefore, the cambium is the source of the tissue system responsible for long-distance transport (the vascular tissue).

questions.text: Ground tissue with no specialized function:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Xylem has the specialized function of transporting water and minerals to different parts of the plant.
Phloem has the specialized function of transporting sucrose and amino acids from source to sink
The main job of the cambium is to promote growth of secondary xylem and phloem. It's located directly between the primary xylem and phloem in a circular layer.
Pith is a source of unspecialized ground tissue that adds structure to a stem at the very center.

questions.explanation: The ground tissue that is correctly described as having no specialized function is the Pith (D).The Pith is the innermost region of the Ground Tissue System in dicot stems. It is composed primarily of large, thin-walled parenchyma cells. Parenchyma cells are considered the least specialized plant cells, involved in generalized functions such as storage. While other ground tissue structures like the cortex may also be non-specialized, in the context of the options provided, the Pith is the most accurate example of non-specialized ground tissue found centrally in the stem, unlike the specialized vascular tissues (xylem, phloem) and the meristematic tissue (cambium).

questions.text: At the venous end of a capillary bed, the osmotic pressure
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Is greater than the hydrostatic pressure
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Osmotic pressure at the venous end of a capillary bed is indeed greater than the hydrostatic pressure. Osmotic pressure is generated by the presence of solutes (such as proteins) in the blood, and it helps to draw fluid back into the capillary.

options.text: Results in a net outflow of fluid
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate for the venous end of a capillary bed. At the venous end, the osmotic pressure is greater than the hydrostatic pressure, leading to a net reabsorption of fluid back into the bloodstream.

options.text: Results in a net reabsorption of fluid into the blood
options.explanation: This statement is correct. At the venous end of a capillary bed, the osmotic pressure is higher than the hydrostatic pressure, resulting in a net reabsorption of fluid from the tissues back into the bloodstream.

The exchange of materials is greatly influenced by the relative balance between the hydrostatic and osmotic pressures of blood and tissue fluids. The osmotic pressure of the surrounding tissue remains constant; however, the hydrostatic pressure at the arterial end is greater than the hydrostatic pressure at the venous end. As a result, fluid moves out of the capillaries at the arterial end and back in at the venous end. Fluid is reabsorbed at the venous end because the osmotic pressure exceeds the hydrostatic pressure

questions.explanation: The exchange of materials is greatly influenced by the relative balance between the hydrostatic and osmotic pressures of blood and tissue fluids. The osmotic pressure of the surrounding tissue remains constant; however, the hydrostatic pressure at the arterial end is greater than the hydrostatic pressure at the venous end. As a result, fluid moves out of the capillaries at the arterial end and back in at the venous end. Fluid is reabsorbed at the venous end because the osmotic pressure exceeds the hydrostatic pressure. (D) is thus the correct answer.

questions.text: An unconscious patient is rushed into the emergency room and needs a fast blood transfusion. Because there is no time to check her medical history or determine her blood type, which type of blood should you, as her doctor, give her?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: People with AB- blood group are not considered universal donors
People with AB+ blood group are not considered universal donors
People with O+ blood group are not considered universal donors

Without knowing a patient’s blood type, the only type of transfusion that we can safely give is O-. People with O- blood are considered universal donors because their blood cells contain no surface antigens. Therefore, O- blood can be given to anyone without causing potentially life threatening consequences.

questions.explanation: Without knowing a patient’s blood type, the only type of transfusion that we can safely give is O-. People with O- blood are considered universal donors because their blood cells contain no surface antigens. Therefore, O- blood can be given to anyone without causing potentially life threatening consequences.

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding arteries and veins?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Arteries are thin walled, muscular, and elastic, whereas veins are thick walled and inelastic
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. Arteries are generally thicker-walled and more muscular and elastic compared to veins. Arteries need to withstand the high pressure generated by the heart's pumping action.

options.text: Arteries always conduct oxygenated blood, whereas veins always carry deoxygenated blood
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. Arteries do typically carry oxygenated blood away from the heart, but there are exceptions such as the pulmonary artery, which carries deoxygenated blood to the lungs. Veins generally carry deoxygenated blood toward the heart, but the pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs to the heart.

options.text: The blood pressure in the aorta is always higher than the pressure in the superior vena cava
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The aorta carries blood away from the heart and has a higher pressure than the superior vena cava, which carries blood back to the heart from the upper part of the body.

options.text: Arteries facilitate blood transport by using skeletal muscle contractions, whereas veins make use of the pumping of the heart to push blood
options.explanation: This statement is not entirely accurate. While arteries and veins both rely on the pumping action of the heart, veins also have one-way valves that aid in preventing backflow and facilitating blood return to the heart, especially against gravity.

questions.explanation: The only answer choice that correctly describes arteries and veins is (C); the pressure in the aorta is usually about 120 or 80 mmHg, depending on whether the heart is in systole or diastole, whereas the pressure in the superior vena cava is extremely low. (A) is wrong because arteries are thick walled and veins are thin walled. (B) is also incorrect; this relationship is reversed in pulmonary circulation. (D) is an opposite answer choice. Arteries make use of the pumping of the heart and the “snapping back” of their elastic walls to transport blood, whereas venous blood is “pumped” by skeletal muscle contractions.

questions.text: At any given time, there is more blood in the venous system than the arterial system.Which of the following features of a vein allows this?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Relative lack of smooth muscle in the wall
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. Veins actually contain smooth muscle in their walls, although less than arteries. However, the presence of smooth muscle in veins helps in the contraction of the vessel walls, aiding in the movement of blood back toward the heart.

Veins have valves that help prevent the backflow of blood. These valves are particularly important in the venous system, where blood is returning to the heart against gravity. The presence of valves facilitates the unidirectional flow of blood and helps maintain venous return.

options.text: Proximity of veins to lymphatic vessels
options.explanation: The proximity of veins to lymphatic vessels is not a primary factor in the fact that there is more blood in the venous system. Lymphatic vessels are part of the lymphatic system, which is separate from the circulatory system.
The endothelial lining of veins is not significantly thinner than that of arteries. Both arteries and veins have endothelial linings that form the inner layer of the vessel walls.

questions.explanation: The relative lack of smooth muscle in venous walls allows stretching to store most of the blood in the body. Valves in the veins allow for one-way flow of blood toward the heart. Both arteries and veins are close to lymphatic vessels, which has no bearing on their relative difference in volume. Both arteries and veins have a thin endothelial lining.

questions.text: Which of the following is involved in the body’s primary blood-buffering mechanism?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While adequate fluid intake is important for maintaining overall health, it is not directly involved in the body's primary blood-buffering mechanism. Fluid intake is more related to maintaining hydration levels.

options.text: Absorption of nutrients in the gastrointestinal system
options.explanation: Nutrient absorption in the gastrointestinal system is essential for providing the body with energy and necessary molecules, but it is not a primary factor in the blood-buffering mechanism.

Carbon dioxide (CO2) plays a crucial role in the body's primary blood-buffering mechanism. CO2 can combine with water in the blood to form carbonic acid (H2CO3), which can dissociate into bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) and hydrogen ions (H+). This bicarbonate ion acts as a buffer, helping to maintain the pH of the blood within a narrow range.

While the kidneys play a role in maintaining acid-base balance, reabsorption in the kidney is not the primary mechanism for buffering blood. The kidneys primarily regulate the excretion of acids and bases to maintain pH balance.

questions.explanation: Carbon dioxide is a by-product of metabolism in cells, which later combines with water to form bicarbonate in a reaction catalyzed by carbonic anhydrase. This system is blood plasma’s most important buffer system. Food and fluid absorption are not significant sources of buffering.

questions.text: The shape of which blood vessel type can be altered in order to redirect blood flow?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Arteries also have a thick lining of smooth muscle, but are generally too large in diameter to be useful in directing blood flow.
This constriction is done by smooth muscle, which is primarily found wrapped around arterioles.
Capillaries have no smooth muscle and cannot be used to direct bloo
Venules may have small layers of smooth muscle, but are not nearly as effective as arterioles.

questions.explanation: Blood vessels can be constricted or dilated in order to adjust blood pressure and reroute blood to areas in need of nutrients and oxygen. This constriction is done by smooth muscle, which is primarily found wrapped around arterioles.Arteries also have a thick lining of smooth muscle, but are generally too large in diameter to be useful in directing blood flow. Capillaries have no smooth muscle and cannot be used to direct blood. Venules may have small layers of smooth muscle, but are not nearly as effective as arterioles.

questions.text: People living at higher altitudes have a higher number of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: People living at higher altitudes often experience lower oxygen levels in the air. In response to this, the body adapts by producing more red blood cells. Red blood cells contain hemoglobin, a protein that binds to oxygen and carries it throughout the body. An increased number of red blood cells helps to enhance oxygen-carrying capacity, allowing individuals to better cope with lower oxygen levels at higher altitudes

Platelets are involved in blood clotting. While living at higher altitudes may not directly impact platelet production, there can be secondary effects. For example, lower oxygen levels might lead to an increase in the risk of blood clotting or other cardiovascular issues. However, this is not a direct result of increased platelet production but rather a response to the environmental condition

White blood cells are a crucial part of the immune system, defending the body against infections. At higher altitudes, there might be an increased risk of exposure to different pathogens, and the body may respond by producing more white blood cells to strengthen the immune system. This adaptation helps individuals living at higher altitudes to better resist infections. However this is not a direct result of living at higher altitudes
Monocytes are a type of white blood cell involved in immune response and inflammation.

questions.explanation: People living at higher altitudes often experience lower oxygen levels in the air. In response to this, the body adapts by producing more red blood cells. Red blood cells contain hemoglobin, a protein that binds to oxygen and carries it throughout the body. An increased number of red blood cells helps to enhance oxygen-carrying capacity, allowing individuals to better cope with lower oxygen levels at higher altitudes

questions.text: In the procedure of blood sample collection, a tube with a top containing heparin is used. The function of heparin in blood sample collection is to
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Heparin does not play a direct role in maintaining blood pH. Blood pH is primarily regulated by the bicarbonate system, respiratory system, and various buffering mechanisms.

Heparin is an anticoagulant, meaning it inhibits blood clotting. In blood sample collection, preventing clotting is essential to obtain a clear and unclotted serum or plasma sample for various laboratory tests. Heparin works by inhibiting the activity of clotting factors and preventing the formation of blood clots.
Heparin is not typically used to prevent blood cell lysis. Anticoagulants like heparin are primarily employed to prevent clotting rather than protecting against blood cell damage.
While heparin helps prevent blood clotting, it is not directly involved in maintaining the overall composition of blood.

questions.explanation: Heparin is an anticoagulant, meaning it inhibits blood clotting.

questions.text: A characteristic feature of the closed circulatory system is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A characteristic feature of the closed circulatory system is the presence of blood vessels, including capillaries. In a closed circulatory system, blood is contained within vessels, and capillaries allow for the exchange of gases, nutrients, and waste products between blood and tissues.

In a closed circulatory system, the blood is circulated within a network of vessels (arteries, veins, and capillaries). The closed system allows for more precise regulation of blood pressure. Blood pressure can be adjusted by the constriction or dilation of blood vessels, and the closed system allows for the maintenance of a consistent pressure gradient for efficient nutrient and gas exchange.

This statement is not necessarily a characteristic feature of a closed circulatory system. Respiratory pigments, such as hemoglobin in vertebrates, can be present in both closed and open circulatory systems. Hemoglobin is responsible for oxygen transport in the blood.

This statement is characteristic of an open circulatory system, not a closed one. In an open circulatory system, such as found in arthropods, the circulatory fluid (hemolymph) bathes the organs directly in open cavities, rather than being confined within vessels. Closed circulatory systems, found in vertebrates, keep the blood within vessels, separating it from the body cavities.

questions.explanation: A characteristic feature of the closed circulatory system is the Regulation of blood pressure (B).In a closed circulatory system (found in all vertebrates, annelids, and cephalopods), blood is entirely contained within vessels, pumped by a heart, and delivered to organs via arteries and capillaries, returning via veins. Since the blood is confined, the circulatory system can maintain a much higher pressure than an open system. This high, controlled pressure ensures that blood is pumped rapidly and efficiently to all tissues, making possible the high metabolic rate and complex body structures of vertebrates.

questions.text: Which of the following statement best describes the function of sinoatrial node?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It sends out electrical impulses to ventrictes to contract.
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the SA node is responsible for initiating and coordinating the contraction of the Atria, not the ventricles

options.text: It is present at upper end of the left atrium
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the SA node is located in the right atrium, near the entrance of the superior vena cava.

options.text: It consists of small number of diffusely oriented cardiac fibers
options.explanation: This option is also incorrect because the SA node consists of a group of specialized cardiac muscle cells, not fibers.

options.text: It sends out electrical impulses to atrial muscles causing both atria to contract.
options.explanation: SA node or the pacemaker is located in the right atrium and it sends out impulses causing both Atria to contract

questions.explanation: Explanation:SA node or the pacemaker is located in the right atrium and it sends out impulses causing both Atria to contract. This option is incorrect because the SA node is responsible for initiating and coordinating the contraction of the Atria, not the ventricles. This option is incorrect because the SA node is located in the right atrium, near the entrance of the superior vena cava. This option is also incorrect because the SA node consists of a group of specialized cardiac muscle cells, not fibers.

questions.text: Antibodies of plasma are manufactured by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Monocytes are immune cells that can develop into macrophages or dendritic cells. They are not directly responsible for manufacturing antibodies; instead, they are involved in phagocytosis and antigen presentation.
Eosinophils are primarily involved in combating parasitic infections and allergic reactions. They do not play a significant role in antibody production.

Plasma cells are differentiated B-lymphocyte white blood cells capable of secreting immunoglobulin or antibodies. These cells play a significant role in the adaptive immune response, namely, being the main cells responsible for humoral immunity.

Neutrophils are the most common type of white blood cells and are essential for immune defense against bacterial infections. However, they do not produce antibodies; their main function is phagocytosis of pathogens.

questions.explanation: Plasma cells are differentiated B-lymphocyte white blood cells capable of secreting immunoglobulin or antibodies. These cells play a significant role in the adaptive immune response, namely, being the main cells responsible for humoral immunity.

questions.text: Duration of one complete heart beat is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Incorrect because the correct answer is 0.8 seconds.
Incorrect because the correct answer is 0.8 seconds.

At a heartbeat 72 beats/minute, duration of each cardiac cycle will be 0.8 seconds. Duration of different stages of the cardiac cycle is given below: Atrial systole: continues for about 0.1 seconds. Ventricular systole: continues for about 0.3 seconds.
Incorrect because the correct answer is 0.8 seconds.

questions.explanation: At a heartbeat 72 beats/minute, duration of each cardiac cycle will be 0.8 seconds. Duration of different stages of the cardiac cycle is given below: Atrial systole: continues for about 0.1 seconds. Ventricular systole: continues for about 0.3 seconds.

questions.text: Which of the following vessels have larger intercellular spaces in their walls?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Is correct as In lymph vessels, intercellular spaces refer to the small gaps or spaces between the endothelial cells that line the walls of these vessels. The endothelial cells form a single layer and create a semi-permeable barrier that allows the movement of fluid, immune cells, and molecules into and out of the lymphatic system.The intercellular spaces allow the entry of interstitial fluid, also known as lymph, into the lymphatic capillaries. These spaces provide a pathway for fluid, solutes, and small molecules to move from the surrounding tissues into the lymphatic system.

Is incorrect as the intercellular spaces of blood capillaries are small The size and permeability of the intercellular spaces in blood capillaries can vary depending on the tissues and their specific requirements. In some tissues, such as the liver and kidneys, the intercellular spaces may be more permeable, allowing for increased exchange of substances. In contrast, in certain specialized capillary beds, such as the blood-brain barrier, the intercellular spaces may be tighter, restricting the passage of larger molecules and maintaining the integrity of the barrier.These spaces allow small molecules, such as gases, ions, and nutrients, to move through the capillary walls
Is incorrect as arterioles, which are small blood vessels that branch out from arteries and lead to capillaries, have limited intercellular spaces between their endothelial cells

Is incorrect as Venules, which are small blood vessels that collect blood from capillaries and merge into veins, do have intercellular spaces between their endothelial cells.the endothelial cells in venule walls are typically connected by intercellular junctions, including tight junctions and adherens junctions. These junctions help maintain the integrity of the vessel wall while allowing for controlled permeability.

questions.explanation: Is correct as the intercellular spaces of blood capillaries are large in a sinusoid capillaries. The size and permeability of the intercellular spaces in blood capillaries can vary depending on the tissues and their specific requirements. In some tissues, such as the liver and kidneys, the intercellular spaces may be more permeable, allowing for increased exchange of substances. In contrast, in certain specialized capillary beds, such as the blood-brain barrier, the intercellular spaces may be tighter, restricting the passage of larger molecules and maintaining the integrity of the barrier.These spaces allow small molecules, such as gases, ions, and nutrients, to move through the capillary walls

questions.text: Elastic fibres are absent in the walls of _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.The aorta is the largest artery in the human body, responsible for distributing oxygen-rich blood from the heart to the rest of the body. It's essentially the main trunk artery from which smaller arteries branch off to supply various organs and tissues

The elastic fibres in the walls of the arteries perform several roles. The first and foremost important function of the elastic fibres is that they help in vascular recoil that enables the blood vessels to stretch when blood flows through them.
The walls of the veins are thin and less elastic than those of the arteries. This is because the veins do not need to be as strong as the arteries as they do not have to withstand high pressure.
Only option C is correct, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The elastic fibres are essential in the recoil and the stretch of the blood vessel in order to survive high blood pressure. In veins, however, due to the lack of significant blood pressure, elastic fibres are not needed.

questions.text: In ECG, QRS wave represents:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The QRS wave occurs just prior to ventricular contraction[ventricular systole] and the T wave occurs when the ventricles are recovering from contraction [ventricular/atrial diastole].
The P wave occurs just prior to atrial contraction [ atrial systole].

This option is incorrect.The QRS wave occurs just prior to ventricular contraction[ventricular systole] and the T wave occurs when the ventricles are recovering from contraction [ventricular/atrial diastole].

This option is incorrect.The QRS wave occurs just prior to ventricular contraction[ventricular systole] and the T wave occurs when the ventricles are recovering from contraction [ventricular/atrial diastole].

questions.explanation: The QRS complex represents depolarization of the ventricles and is followed by ventricular contraction which is collectively known as the ventricular systole.

questions.text: Serum differs from blood in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Globulins are proteins in the blood, including antibodies, and are present in both serum and plasma. Serum contains globulins, so this option is incorrect.
Albumins are vital proteins for maintaining osmotic pressure and are present in both serum and plasma. Serum is not lacking albumins, making this option incorrect.
Serum is derived from blood plasma and lacks clotting factors, such as fibrinogen, which are used during coagulation. Therefore, this option is correct.
Serum contains antibodies, which are important immune proteins and a type of globulin. Antibodies are present in serum, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Serum is plasma without clotting proteins like fibrinogen, which are consumed during coagulation.

questions.text: Which one of the following proteins takes part in blood clotting
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Prothrombin is a protein made by the liver. Prothrombin helps blood to clot. The "prothrombin time" (PT) is one way of measuring how long it takes blood to form a clot, and it is measured in seconds (such as 13.2 seconds). A normal PT indicates that a normal amount of blood-clotting protein is available.

Fibrinogen is a protein produced by the liver. This protein helps stop bleeding by helping blood clots to form. When blood clotting is activated, fibrinogen circulating in the blood is converted to fibrin which in turn helps in forming a stable clot at the site of vascular disruption.
Immunoglobulins, also known as antibodies, are glycoprotein molecules produced by plasma cells (white blood cells). Immunoglobulins play a key role in the body's immune system.
Globulins are a group of proteins in blood. They are made in liver by the immune system.

Prothrombin is a protein made by the liver. Prothrombin helps blood to clot. The "prothrombin time" (PT) is one way of measuring how long it takes blood to form a clot, and it is measured in seconds (such as 13.2 seconds). A normal PT indicates that a normal amount of blood-clotting protein is available.Fibrinogen is a protein produced by the liver. This protein helps stop bleeding by helping blood clots to form. When blood clotting is activated, fibrinogen circulating in the blood is converted to fibrin which in turn helps in forming a stable clot at the site of vascular disruption.

questions.explanation: Prothrombin is a protein made by the liver. Prothrombin helps blood to clot. The "prothrombin time" (PT) is one way of measuring how long it takes blood to form a clot, and it is measured in seconds (such as 13.2 seconds). A normal PT indicates that a normal amount of blood-clotting protein is available.Fibrinogen is a protein produced by the liver. This protein helps stop bleeding by helping blood clots to form. When blood clotting is activated, fibrinogen circulating in the blood is converted to fibrin which in turn helps in forming a stable clot at the site of vascular disruption.

questions.text: The blood flow in milliliters/ minute during exercise to the skin is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) 1500 ml: This option states that the blood flow to the skin during exercise is 1500 milliliters per minute. However, this is not the correct answer.
b) 1600 ml: This option states that the blood flow to the skin during exercise is 1600 milliliters per minute. However, this is not the correct answer.
c) 1800 ml: This option states that the blood flow to the skin during exercise is 1800 milliliters per minute. However, this is not the correct answer.

d) 1900 ml: This option states that the blood flow to the skin during exercise is 1900 milliliters per minute. This is the correct answer. During exercise, blood flow to the skin increases to help regulate body temperature and dissipate heat.

questions.explanation: Skin blood flow plays a major role in the distribution of thermal energy during exercise. When we exercise, our body temperature increases and carries the blood toward the skin’s surface, causing us to sweat and cool off. This natural body mechanism can lead to a flushed, red face, which can be especially more noticeable in fair-skinned individuals. The blood flow to the skin during exercise is 1900 ml/min. orMore oxygen is required by the cells as the rate of respiration increases, thus the blood flow to the skin increases as well.REFERENCE: KPK BIOLOGY BOOK

questions.text: Amount of O2 carried by red blood cells is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) 77%: This option is incorrect. The amount of oxygen carried by red blood cells (RBCs) is much higher than 77%. If RBCs carried only 77% of oxygen, it would mean that a significant amount of oxygen would remain untransported, leading to severe oxygen deprivation in the body.

b) 90%: This option is incorrect. While RBCs do play a crucial role in transporting oxygen, they do not carry as much as 90% of the total oxygen in the blood. The percentage of oxygen carried by RBCs is higher than this.
c) 87%: This option is incorrect. Similar to option b, the percentage of oxygen carried by RBCs is higher than 87%. RBCs are specialized for oxygen transport, and they do so efficiently.

d) 97%: This is the correct option. RBCs contain hemoglobin, a protein that binds with oxygen in the lungs and carries it throughout the body. Approximately 97% of oxygen is bound to hemoglobin in RBCs and transported to various tissues and organs where oxygen is needed for cellular respiration and energy production.

questions.explanation: Red blood cells (also known as erythrocytes) contain a protein called hemoglobin, which carries oxygen from the lungs to all parts of the body. Haemoglobin is an iron-containing protein that is found within all RBCs. Haemoglobin is made of four subunits: two alpha subunits and two beta subunits. Each subunit surrounds a central heme group that contains iron and binds one oxygen molecule, allowing each hemoglobin molecule to bind four oxygen molecules, forming oxyhemoglobin. Approximately, 97% of oxygen is carried by the red blood cells as oxyhemoglobin, while 3% is transported as dissolved oxygen in the plasma.

questions.text: Blood pressure towards the brain during rest hours is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) 850mm/minute: This option suggests that blood pressure towards the brain during rest hours is 850 mm per minute. However, blood pressure is typically measured in millimeters of mercury (mmHg), not in mm per minute. The unit "mm per minute" is not commonly used to express blood pressure.

b) 900mm/minute: This option suggests that blood pressure towards the brain during rest hours is 900 mm per minute. As mentioned earlier, blood pressure is typically measured in mmHg, not in mm per minute.

Cerebral circulation refers to the flow of blood through the network of blood vessels supplying the brain. The arteries deliver oxygenated blood, glucose, and other nutrients to the brain and the veins carry deoxygenated blood back to the heart, removing carbon dioxide, lactic acid, and other metabolic products. The four main arteries that supply blood to our brain are the left and right internal carotid arteries and the left and right vertebral arteries. When cerebral circulation is impaired, less oxygen and glucose reach the brain. This can cause brain damage and neurological problems. The rate of blood flow toward the brain during rest is 750ml/minute.

d) 730mm/minute: This option suggests that blood pressure towards the brain during rest hours is 730 mm per minute. As with the previous options, blood pressure is usually measured in mmHg, not in mm per minute.

questions.explanation: Cerebral circulation refers to the flow of blood through the network of blood vessels supplying the brain. The arteries deliver oxygenated blood, glucose, and other nutrients to the brain and the veins carry deoxygenated blood back to the heart, removing carbon dioxide, lactic acid, and other metabolic products. The four main arteries that supply blood to our brain are the left and right internal carotid arteries and the left and right vertebral arteries. When cerebral circulation is impaired, less oxygen and glucose reach the brain. This can cause brain damage and neurological problems. The rate of blood flow toward the brain during rest is 750ml/minute.This is a value to be memorized from the following table given in KPK Biology Book 1.

questions.text: Cardiac output is increased by all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Acute hypoxia exposure increases heart rate and cardiac output via B-adrenergic stimulation, and can result in high blood pressure and rate pressure product.
During exercise, more blood is sent to the active skeletal muscles, and, as body temperature increases, more blood is sent to the skin.
Sleep decreases cardiac output.
During pregnancy, your blood volume increases by 30-50% to nourish a growing baby, the heart pumps more blood, and hence heart output increases.
The presence of anemia activates the sympathetic nervous system, which results in an increased heart rate.

questions.explanation: Sleep decreases the cardiac output.
